mnemonics.title: Chandy-Lamport Marker Rules
mnemonics.content: FIRST MARKER: Record your state, start capturing channel. SUBSEQUENT MARKERS: Stop capturing that channel. This rule ensures all in-transit messages are captured correctly.

mnemonics.title: Consistent State Properties
mnemonics.content: A consistent global state has two key properties: (1) SAFETY - 'something bad never happens' - no message received without being sent, and (2) LIVENESS - 'something good eventually happens' - the snapshot can be completed.

mnemonics.title: Termination Detection
mnemonics.content: For termination: BOTH (a) all processes locally terminated (passive) AND (b) all channels empty (no in-transit messages). Both conditions are necessary!

mnemonics.title: Happens-Before Definition
mnemonics.content: → is defined by: (1) Same process order, (2) Send→Receive of same message, (3) Transitivity. Concurrent events are those where neither a→b nor b→a.

acronyms.usage: Channel property required by Chandy-Lamport - messages arrive in order sent
The state of a process at a point in logical time (variables, program counter)
A model where global state detection is often studied
A cut that respects the happens-before relation

visualTips.visualization: Draw processes as vertical lines, messages as diagonal lines connecting them. The 'cut' is a line that may zigzag across processes - it's consistent if no message goes backward through the cut.
Think of channels as pipes. Messages 'in transit' are balls inside the pipe at snapshot time. The marker 'drains' the pipe - messages before marker = in-transit; after marker = not captured.
Imagine dropping a stone (initiator) in a pond - ripples (markers) spread outward. As each ripple reaches a point, it captures the state at that moment. The ripples eventually cover all processes.
Consistent = family photo where everyone is looking at the camera. Inconsistent = someone blinked (receive happened) but no one opened eyes (send didn't happen).

analogies.analogy: Like taking a photo of a moving crowd with slow shutter speed - you're capturing where everyone was at approximately the same time, not exactly the same instant.
Like letters in the postal system - they've been sent but not yet delivered. The snapshot must 'catch' these letters to be complete.
Like a wave at the beach - it sweeps forward. Any message crossing the wave goes forward (future to past), not backward. If it went backward, we'd have an inconsistency.
Like a checkpoint in a marathon race - the marker tells runners 'this is where you were when I passed'. Anyone after the marker wasn't there yet.
